description: Deep reference for core data structures — covers operations, variants, traps, and disqualifiers for each structure family.
usage: Use when two candidate paths share the same algorithmic paradigm. The data structure chosen determines the constants, access patterns, mutation cost, and feasibility.

families.name: Heap / Priority Queue
families.coreContract: Insert in O(log n); extract the minimum (or maximum) in O(log n); peek in O(1). Does not support arbitrary access or update without augmentation.
whenToPrefer: You repeatedly need the globally best element (min or max) from a changing set
Dijkstra-style 'process cheapest frontier node next'
K-way merging, top-K queries, sliding window maximum without monotonic queue

variants.name: Binary min/max heap
variants.characteristic: Standard; O(log n) push/pop
variants.bestUsedWhen: General priority queue needs

variants.name: Indexed heap (heap + position map)
variants.characteristic: O(log n) decrease-key by tracking positions
variants.bestUsedWhen: Dijkstra with edge relaxation, Prim's MST

variants.name: Two-heap partition
variants.characteristic: One min-heap + one max-heap sharing a population
variants.bestUsedWhen: Median of a data stream, range median

variants.name: Monotonic deque (deque-as-heap)
variants.characteristic: Maintains sorted order by evicting dominated elements
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sliding window max/min in O(n) total

variants.name: Lazy deletion heap
variants.characteristic: Stale entries left in heap; validity checked at pop time
variants.bestUsedWhen: Interval scheduling, task cooldown, sweep line events

variants.name: K-way merge heap
variants.characteristic: One entry per input stream; pop + re-insert from same stream
variants.bestUsedWhen: Merge K sorted lists, external sort

implementationTraps: Python's heapq is min-heap only — negate values for max-heap; don't forget to negate back
Lazy deletion requires a 'seen' set or a version counter; forgetting this causes incorrect pops
Two-heap median: rebalancing invariant (size difference <= 1) must be maintained on every insert and remove
Indexed heap position map must be updated on every swap during sift-up/sift-down, not just at insert
disqualifiers: Need to find an arbitrary element (use hash map instead)
Need sorted iteration over the full set (use BST or sort)
The set is static — just sort once

families.name: Tree Variants
families.coreContract: A family of hierarchical structures offering O(log n) search, insert, and delete on ordered data, plus range queries. The specific variant determines what additional operations are O(log n) vs O(n).
whenToPrefer: Data is ordered and you need both point queries and range queries
Dynamic set with frequent insertions/deletions (vs. static -> just sort)
Need rank queries ('how many elements <= X?') or order statistics

variants.name: BST (unbalanced)
variants.characteristic: Simple; degrades to O(n) on sorted input
variants.bestUsedWhen: Never in production; useful for teaching

variants.name: AVL / Red-Black tree
variants.characteristic: Self-balancing; O(log n) guaranteed
variants.bestUsedWhen: Ordered map/set (most language std libs)

variants.name: Segment tree
variants.characteristic: Each node covers a range; supports range query + point update
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range sum/min/max with point updates

variants.name: Segment tree with lazy propagation
variants.characteristic: Deferred range updates; O(log n) range update + range query
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range add + range sum, range set + range min

variants.name: Fenwick tree (BIT)
variants.characteristic: Implicit tree via index bit tricks; smaller constant than segment tree
variants.bestUsedWhen: Prefix sum with point update; 2D variants for grid

variants.name: Trie (prefix tree)
variants.characteristic: Characters at edges; O(L) ops where L = string length
variants.bestUsedWhen: Prefix matching, autocomplete, XOR maximization

variants.name: Persistent segment tree
variants.characteristic: Each update creates a new root; old versions accessible
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range kth element, versioned data, offline queries

variants.name: Merge sort tree
variants.characteristic: Segment tree where each node stores a sorted array of its range
variants.bestUsedWhen: Count elements in range [L,R] satisfying a predicate

variants.name: K-d tree
variants.characteristic: Partitions k-dimensional space alternating on each axis
variants.bestUsedWhen: Nearest neighbor in 2D/3D, range search in space

variants.name: Cartesian tree / Treap
variants.characteristic: Heap-ordered by priority, BST-ordered by key; randomized balance
variants.bestUsedWhen: Implicit sequence with split/merge, rope data structure

implementationTraps: Segment tree: 1-indexed vs 0-indexed; size must be next power of 2 or use recursive build
Lazy propagation: the lazy tag must be pushed down before recursing into children, not after
Fenwick tree: update(i, delta) and query(i) are both 1-indexed; off-by-one errors are common
Trie: remember to handle end-of-word markers separately from the existence of a child node
Persistent structures: copying the path (O(log n) nodes) not the whole tree
disqualifiers: Updates are rare and queries are batch -> sort offline instead
Data is unordered and you only need exact-match lookup -> hash map is faster and simpler
N is small (< 1000) -> just use a sorted array with binary search

families.name: Hash-Based Structures
families.coreContract: O(1) average insert, delete, and lookup. No ordering. Worst-case O(n) due to collisions, but rare with good hash functions.
whenToPrefer: Fast exact-match lookup is the bottleneck
Counting frequencies, detecting duplicates, memoizing seen states
The key space is large but the active set is sparse

variants.name: HashMap / dictionary
variants.characteristic: Key -> value; O(1) average
variants.bestUsedWhen: Any frequency count, index lookup, memo table

variants.name: HashSet
variants.characteristic: Membership only; O(1) average
variants.bestUsedWhen: Visited set in BFS, duplicate detection

variants.name: Counting array
variants.characteristic: Fixed-range keys (e.g., chars, small ints); O(1) true worst-case
variants.bestUsedWhen: Character frequency, bucket sort

variants.name: Rolling hash
variants.characteristic: Hash of a sliding window updated incrementally
variants.bestUsedWhen: Rabin-Karp substring search, longest duplicate substring

variants.name: Polynomial hash / string hashing
variants.characteristic: Map strings to integers for O(1) comparison
variants.bestUsedWhen: Anagram grouping, palindrome detection over substrings

variants.name: Two-sum complement map
variants.characteristic: Store seen values; for each new value check if complement exists
variants.bestUsedWhen: Two-sum, subarray sum equals K (prefix sum + map)

variants.name: Coordinate compression + map
variants.characteristic: Map large sparse values to dense indices
variants.bestUsedWhen: Segment tree on large value range, offline range queries

implementationTraps: Rolling hash collisions: use double hashing (two independent moduli) to reduce false positives
Python dicts preserve insertion order (3.7+) — don't rely on this in language-agnostic solutions
Counting array bounds: ensure the array is sized for the full input range, not just what you've seen
Coordinate compression: compress after reading all input; queries must use the same mapping as updates
disqualifiers: Need to iterate in sorted order (use BST or sorted array)
Need range queries ('all keys between X and Y') -> hash maps can't do this
Adversarial inputs can cause hash collisions -> use randomized hash or tree map

families.name: Graph Representations
families.coreContract: Encodes vertices and edges; the representation chosen governs the cost of the operations most frequently used (adjacency test, neighbor enumeration, edge weight lookup).

variants.name: Adjacency list
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sparse graphs; most real problems

variants.name: Adjacency matrix
variants.characteristic: Space O(V^2); adjacency test O(1); neighbor list O(V)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Dense graphs; Floyd-Warshall; small V

variants.name: Edge list
variants.characteristic: Space O(E); adjacency test O(E); neighbor list O(E)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Kruskal's MST; sorting edges by weight

variants.name: Implicit graph
variants.characteristic: Space O(1) storage; computed on-the-fly
variants.bestUsedWhen: BFS/DFS on grid, state-space search

variants.name: CSR (Compressed Sparse Row)
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(log degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Read-only graphs; cache-efficient traversal

variants.name: Multigraph with edge ids
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Multiple edges between same nodes; flow networks

implicitGraphPatterns: Grid as graph: cell (r, c) is a node; 4/8 neighbors are edges
State as node: (position, remaining_fuel, keys_held) is a node in BFS
Interval as node: edge from interval A to B if A ends before B starts (interval scheduling graph)
Bitmask as node: each subset is a node; DP on subsets is BFS on hypercube graph
implementationTraps: Directed vs. undirected: adding edge u->v in an undirected graph requires also adding v->u
Self-loops and parallel edges: adjacency matrix handles them naturally; adjacency list needs care
Implicit graphs: ensure the neighbor-generation function is correct for boundary cells (grid edges)
Graph of intervals: when converting intervals to a DAG, ensure you sort by start time first
disqualifiers: Problem is fundamentally about ordered sequences, not relationships -> don't force a graph
V is huge but edges are implicit and acyclic -> DP on the implicit DAG is cleaner than building the graph

families.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
families.coreContract: Two operations — union(a, b) merges two sets; find(a) returns the canonical representative of a's set. With union-by-rank and path compression: both operations are effectively O(alpha(n)) ~ O(1).
whenToPrefer: Dynamic connectivity: 'are these two nodes in the same component after adding edges?'
Detecting cycles while building a spanning tree (Kruskal's)
Offline connectivity queries where edges only appear, never disappear

variants.name: Standard DSU
variants.characteristic: Union by rank + path compression
variants.bestUsedWhen: Component membership, Kruskal's MST

variants.name: Weighted/labeled DSU
variants.characteristic: Each node stores a value relative to its parent
variants.bestUsedWhen: Accounts with transfers, relative distances

variants.name: Rollback DSU (link-cut)
variants.characteristic: Union by rank without path compression; supports undo
variants.bestUsedWhen: Offline dynamic connectivity, divide-and-conquer on edges

variants.name: DSU on tree (small-to-large)
variants.characteristic: Merge smaller child's data into larger child's; O(n log n) total
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subtree queries, color counting on trees

variants.name: Bipartite checking DSU
variants.characteristic: Two nodes per vertex (true/false state); union checks consistency
variants.bestUsedWhen: 2-colorability, odd cycle detection

implementationTraps: Path compression alone (without union by rank) gives O(log n) not O(alpha(n))
Rollback DSU: path compression breaks rollback — must use union by rank only
Weighted DSU: when doing path compression, the weights along the path must be composed correctly
Off-by-one in initialization: parent[i] = i for all nodes, including isolated ones
disqualifiers: Edges can be removed as well as added -> DSU doesn't support deletions; use link-cut trees or offline processing
Need to enumerate all members of a component -> DSU only gives representative, not membership list
Need shortest path -> DSU knows connectivity but not distance

families.name: Monotonic Stack and Queue
families.coreContract: A stack or deque maintained in sorted order by evicting elements that can never be the answer to a future query. Each element is pushed and popped at most once -> O(n) total across all operations.
whenToPrefer: 'Next greater / smaller element' for each position
Sliding window maximum or minimum in O(n)
Largest rectangle / container problems
Problems where earlier elements become irrelevant once a better candidate appears

variants.name: Monotonic increasing stack
variants.characteristic: Bottom to top: increasing
variants.bestUsedWhen: Next smaller element, largest rectangle in histogram

variants.name: Monotonic decreasing stack
variants.characteristic: Bottom to top: decreasing
variants.bestUsedWhen: Next greater element, stock span problem

variants.name: Monotonic deque (sliding window)
variants.characteristic: Front is current best; rear evicts dominated elements
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sliding window max/min, maximum of all subarrays of size K

variants.name: Two-stack queue
variants.characteristic: Two stacks simulate O(1) amortized queue with min/max tracking
variants.bestUsedWhen: Min-queue (queue supporting O(1) minimum queries)

variants.name: Cartesian tree construction
variants.characteristic: Monotonic stack builds the Cartesian tree in O(n)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range minimum query structure, treap-like problems

implementationTraps: Deciding what to store: often store indices, not values, so you can compute spans/widths
Equality handling: 'strictly greater' vs 'greater or equal' changes which duplicates survive
Sliding window deque: evict from the front when the window slides past an element, from the rear when a new element dominates
Off-by-one in the span calculation: right - left - 1 not right - left when both pointers are exclusive
disqualifiers: Window size changes non-monotonically (deque no longer sufficient; use segment tree)
Need arbitrary access into the 'stack' by value (use a BST instead)
Query isn't about a contiguous range or a sliding window

families.name: Sliding Window and Two Pointer
families.coreContract: Maintain a contiguous subarray (sliding window) or two indices moving toward each other (two pointer) to answer queries in O(n) rather than O(n^2).
whenToPrefer: 'Longest/shortest subarray satisfying a property'
'Pair of elements summing to a target in a sorted array'
Constraint on a subarray is monotone: expanding the window can only make it harder (or easier) to satisfy

variants.name: Fixed-size window
variants.characteristic: Right advances; left = right - k
variants.bestUsedWhen: Average of subarrays of size K, max sum of size K

variants.name: Variable-size window (shrink when invalid)
variants.characteristic: Right advances freely; left advances when constraint violated
variants.bestUsedWhen: Longest substring without repeating chars, min window substring

variants.name: Two pointer on sorted array
variants.characteristic: Left from start, right from end; move based on comparison
variants.bestUsedWhen: Two-sum in sorted array, container with most water

variants.name: Two pointer on linked list
variants.characteristic: Fast and slow pointers
variants.bestUsedWhen: Cycle detection, find middle node, Kth from end

variants.name: Multi-pointer (three pointers)
variants.characteristic: One anchor + two-pointer sweep
variants.bestUsedWhen: Three-sum, four-sum

variants.name: Prefix sum + two pointer
variants.characteristic: Precompute prefix sums; binary search or two-pointer on them
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subarray sum >= K (positive values), minimum size subarray sum

implementationTraps: Variable window: the shrink condition must restore validity fully before recording an answer
Two pointer on unsorted data: requires sorting first — the sort cost O(n log n) must be acceptable
Window with a frequency map: decrement the map when the left pointer moves; don't delete keys, check count == 0
Sliding window on circular arrays: run two passes or use modular indexing
disqualifiers: Constraint is not monotone (e.g., 'subarray with sum exactly K' on mixed-sign input -> prefix sum + hash map instead)
Array is unsorted and sorting would destroy required ordering
Queries are offline and non-contiguous (use segment tree or BIT)

families.name: Bit Manipulation and Bitsets
families.coreContract: Operate on integers as arrays of bits. Modern CPUs execute bitwise ops in O(1) on 64-bit words; bitsets compress boolean arrays by 64x, enabling O(n/64) set operations.
whenToPrefer: State is a subset of a small universe (N <= 20 for full bitmask DP; N <= 10^6 for bitset)
Need fast set intersection, union, or complement
Parity, power-of-two, or lowest-set-bit tricks replace arithmetic

variants.name: Bitmask DP
variants.characteristic: State = bitmask of chosen items; transition flips bits
variants.bestUsedWhen: TSP, set cover, assignment with N <= 20

variants.name: XOR trick
variants.characteristic: a ^ b ^ b = a; XOR of a pair cancels
variants.bestUsedWhen: Find single non-duplicate, missing number, swap without temp

variants.name: Lowest set bit (LSB)
variants.characteristic: x & (-x) isolates the rightmost 1
variants.bestUsedWhen: Fenwick tree indexing, iterating over set bits

variants.name: Popcount
variants.characteristic: bin(x).count('1') or hardware POPCNT
variants.bestUsedWhen: Hamming distance, count set bits, parity

variants.name: Bit shifting for powers
variants.bestUsedWhen: Fast multiply/divide by powers of 2, flag masks

variants.name: Bitset DP
variants.characteristic: Use Python int or C++ bitset<N> as a dense boolean array
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subset sum feasibility for large N, reachability in boolean matrix

variants.name: Gray code
variants.characteristic: Consecutive values differ by exactly one bit
variants.bestUsedWhen: Generating permutations/subsets without revisiting, rotary encoders

implementationTraps: Signed integer overflow: 1 << 31 is undefined in C++ with int; use 1LL << 31
Python integers are arbitrary precision — bitmask DP works for N > 64, but gets slow
XOR swap: a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b fails when a and b are the same variable (same memory)
Iterating over all subsets of a mask: sub = mask; while sub > 0: process(sub); sub = (sub-1) & mask — don't forget to process sub = 0 separately if needed
disqualifiers: N > 20 for bitmask DP (2^20 = 1M states is borderline; 2^25 is too large)
Operations require ordering or range queries (bits don't have positional meaning beyond index)
Problem involves arithmetic that doesn't reduce to bit tricks

crossStructureDecisionGuide.bottleneckOperation: Range query + point update on array
Exact membership / frequency of arbitrary keys
Sliding window aggregate in linear time
